{"parts":[{"tool_name":"run_bash_command","content":{"stdout":"# Hiring POV: Test Engineer @ DebugCorp

## Role Overview
This is a debugging-centric test engineering role at a company where finding and fixing problems is core to the identity. They want a self-directed Python automation engineer who thinks like a detective — someone who doesn't just write tests but actively hunts bugs and builds systems to catch them. The sparse JD signals a lean team that values initiative over instruction-following.

## Explicit Requirements
- 3+ years Python experience
- CI/CD experience
- Strong debugging skills
- Able to work remotely

## Implicit Filters
- Self-directed workers who don't need hand-holding (sparse JD signals they expect candidates to figure things out)
- Tool-agnostic adaptability (no specific CI/CD tools mentioned = they want problem-solvers, not tool operators)
- Comfort with ambiguity (minimal JD detail suggests fast-moving environment)
- Debugging-first mindset over checkbox testing (company literally named DebugCorp)

## Trait Signals
- Analytical problem-solver who enjoys root cause investigation
- Direct, efficient communicator (JD brevity mirrors expected communication style)
- Self-starter who thrives without detailed specifications
- Curious investigator who digs deeper than surface symptoms
- Automation-minded (CI/CD requirement signals preference for systematic approaches)

## Red Flags
- Manual-only testing background without automation experience
- Candidates who need extensive documentation or hand-holding to be productive
- Those who describe testing as 'following test cases' rather than 'finding problems'
- Anyone who struggles to articulate debugging methodology or past debugging wins
- Candidates asking excessive clarifying questions before demonstrating initiative

## Story Types Valued
- Root cause analysis stories where they traced a bug through multiple system layers
- Building or significantly improving CI/CD pipelines
- Finding elusive, hard-to-reproduce bugs that others had given up on
- Debugging production incidents under pressure
- Catching critical bugs before release that would have caused major issues
- Creating automated testing systems that prevented regression classes

## Validation Questions
- Walk me through how you'd debug a flaky test that fails 10% of the time
- Describe a bug that took you the longest to find — what made it hard?
- How have you improved CI/CD pipeline reliability or speed in previous roles?
- Tell me about a time you found a bug that wasn't in your area — how did you approach it?
- What's your process when you inherit a codebase with poor test coverage?

## Culture Markers
- Lean/efficient (minimal JD = minimal bureaucracy likely)
- Remote-tolerant but not remote-first (suggests some in-office culture exists)
- Technical depth valued over process adherence
- Debugging is identity-level important (it's in the company name)
- Pragmatic over formal (no corporate language, no mission statement fluff)

## Missing Information
- Compensation range and benefits
- Team size and structure (solo QA or part of a testing team?)
- Product domain and tech stack details
- Types of testing emphasized (unit/integration/e2e/performance)
- Specific CI/CD tools in use (Jenkins, GitHub Actions, CircleCI, etc.)
- Growth path and career development opportunities
- Interview process and timeline
- Why the role is open (growth vs backfill)
- On-call or production support expectations
